questionText: A curve has parametric equations $x = \cos 2t$ and $y = \sin t$, for $0 \le t < 2\pi$. (a) Find a Cartesian equation of the curve in the form $x = f(y)$, and state the domain of $x$ and the range of $y$. (b) The line $x = 2y$ meets the curve. Find the exact coordinates of the point(s) of intersection, explaining why any spurious solution is rejected.

steps.explanation: Because $x$ is a double angle and $y = \sin t$, choose the form of $\cos 2t$ written in terms of $\sin t$.
This version uses only $\sin t$, which we already know equals $y$.
Replacing $\cos 2t$ by $x$ and $\sin t$ by $y$ eliminates $t$ and gives $x$ as a function of $y$.
This is a parabola on its side, opening in the negative-$x$ direction with vertex at $(1, 0)$.
As a cosine, $\cos 2t$ stays within $[-1, 1]$, so $x$ is confined to $-1 \le x \le 1$.
As a sine, $y$ ranges over $[-1, 1]$. These bounds matter when we check the solutions later.
At an intersection both equations hold, so we can equate the two expressions for $x$.
This single equation in $y$ locates the parameter values where the line meets the curve.
Bringing all terms to one side gives a standard quadratic in $y$.
The quadratic does not factorise neatly, so we use the formula with $a=2$, $b=2$, $c=-1$.
Since $\sqrt{12} = 2\sqrt{3}$, dividing top and bottom by $2$ tidies the two candidate values.
Numerical values help us test them against the allowed range for $y$.
Because $y = \sin t$ can never be less than $-1$, this value is impossible on the curve, so we discard it.
This value, about $0.366$, lies safely inside $[-1, 1]$, so it corresponds to a genuine point on the curve.
Using the line $x = 2y$ gives the matching $x$-coordinate.
This single point, approximately $(0.73, 0.37)$, is the only place the line $x = 2y$ meets the curve.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: (a) $x = 1 - 2y^{2}$, with domain $-1 \le x \le 1$ and range $-1 \le y \le 1$. (b) The only intersection is $\left(\sqrt{3}-1,\ \dfrac{\sqrt{3}-1}{2}\right)$; the root $y = \dfrac{-1-\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx -1.37$ is rejected because $y = \sin t$ must satisfy $-1 \le y \le 1$.
